It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Clicking on the blue circle moves the nearest magenta square INTO the blue circle! - Magenta square jumped from (29-30, 26-27) to (26-27, 32-33) - now inside the blue circle - This is the key mechanic! The goal is likely to move all magenta squares into the blue circle - Used 27 actions, target is 42
- Plan: Click on the blue circle repeatedly to move all remaining magenta squares into it.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Hmm, clicking on the blue circle again didn't move another magenta square. The positions are the same: [(1, 2, 5, 6), (26, 27, 32, 33), (35, 36, 16, 17), (54, 55, 45, 46), (56, 57, 15, 16)].
So clicking on the blue circle only moved the magenta square that was already near it. The other magenta squares are still far away.
This suggests that I need to first move the magenta squares close to the blue circle, and then clicking on the blue circle will "absorb" them.
So the process is:
1. Move magenta squares towards the blue circle by clicking their top-left cells (which moves them up-left)
2. When a magenta square is close enough to the blue circle, clicking on the blue circle will move it into the blue circle
Let me continue moving the other magenta squares towards the blue circle.
The remaining magenta squares are at:
- (35-36, 16-17) - needs to move up-left to reach blue circle area
- (54-55, 45-46) - needs to move up-left to reach blue circle area
- (56-57, 15-16) - needs to move up-left to reach blue circle area
The blue circle is at rows 23-31, cols 29-37.
For (35-36, 16-17): needs to move up about 4-6 rows and right about 13 cols... but it can only move up-left, so it would move away from the blue circle's right side.
Wait, this doesn't make sense. If the magenta squares can only move up-left, then squares that are to the left of the blue circle would move further left, away from the blue circle.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the mechanic is different. Let me look at the positions again:
- (35-36, 16-17) - this is to the left of the blue circle (cols 16-17 vs 29-37)
- (54-55, 45-46) - this is to the right of the blue circle (cols 45-46 vs 29-37)
- (56-57, 15-16) - this is to the left of the blue circle (cols 15-16 vs 29-37)
If I move (35-36, 16-17) up-left, it would move to (34-35, 15-16), then (33-34, 14-15), etc. This would move it further left, away from the blue circle.
But if I move (54-55, 45-46) up-left, it would move to (53-54, 44-45), then (52-53, 43-44), etc. This would move it up and left, towards the blue circle.
So the squares to the right of the blue circle can be moved towards it, but the squares to the left cannot.
Hmm, maybe I need to move the squares differently. Let me think about this.
Actually, wait. Looking at the previous moves, the magenta square at (38-39, 35-36) was moved up-left to (29-30, 26-27), and then clicking on the blue circle moved it to (26-27, 32-33), which is inside the blue circle.
So the process is:
1. Move magenta squares up-left until they're close to the blue circle
2. Click on the blue circle to "absorb" them
But for squares that are to the left of the blue circle, moving them up-left would move them further away. So maybe I need to move them up-left until they're at the right row, and then... hmm, but they can only move up-left.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the squares don't need to be moved up-left to reach the blue circle. Maybe I need to move them up-left until they're at the right row, and then clicking on the blue circle will move them to the center.
Let me test this by moving the square at (35-36, 16-17) up-left a few times and then clicking on the blue circle.
Actually, wait. The square at (35-36, 16-17) is at rows 35-36, which is below the blue circle (rows 23-31). If I move it up-left, it would move to rows 34-35, then 33-34, etc. Eventually, it would reach rows 31-32, which is close to the blue circle's bottom row (31).
But the column would also move left, from 16-17 to 15-16, then 14-15, etc. This would move it further left, away from the blue circle's columns (29-37).
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me try a different approach. Let me move the square at (54-55, 45-46) up-left, since it's to the right of the blue circle and moving it up-left would bring it closer.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model:
- Clicking on blue circle only absorbed the magenta square that was already near it
- Other magenta squares are still far away
- Need to move remaining magenta squares close to blue circle first, then click blue circle to absorb them
- Squares to the right of blue circle can be moved towards it (up-left)
- Squares to the left of blue circle would move further left (away) when moved up-left
- Used 28 actions, target is 42
Plan: Move the magenta square at (54-55, 45-46) towards the blue circle since it's to the right and moving up-left brings it closer.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move the magenta square at (54-55, 45-46) towards the blue circle
# Blue circle at rows 23-31, cols 29-37
# Square at rows 54-55, cols 45-46
# Need to move it up about 23-24 rows and left about 16-17 cols
# But that would take many actions... let me move it a few times and see

# Actually, let me first check if there's a faster way
# Let me move it up-left several times and see if clicking blue circle absorbs it earlier

# Move it 5 times up-left
actions = []
for i in range(5):
    actions.append({'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 54 - i, 'col': 45 - i})

result = action(actions)

after = current_frame.segmentation
m_positions = []
for n in after['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        b = n['boundary']
        r = (min([p[0] for p in b]), max([p[0] for p in b]), min([p[1] for p in b]), max([p[1] for p in b]))
        m_positions.append(r)

print(f"Magenta positions: {sorted(m_positions)}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
